title: Email Writing
subtitle: Write professional emails for work and personal use
overview: Master formal and informal email writing for Indian workplace and personal scenarios. Learn structure, tone, and templates for common situations like job applications, leave requests, complaints, and client communication.

content_blocks.content: Every professional email follows a standard structure: Subject Line → Greeting → Body (Introduction, Main Content, Closing) → Sign-off → Signature. The subject line should be clear and specific. Use proper greetings (Dear Sir/Madam for formal, Hi/Hello for semi-formal). Keep paragraphs short (2-3 sentences) for readability.

content_blocks.title: Well-Structured Professional Email
content_blocks.content:
Subject: Request for Project Deadline Extension

Dear Mr. Sharma,

I am writing regarding the marketing campaign project currently due on 25th June. Due to unexpected client feedback requiring major revisions, I request a 5-day extension.

I have completed 80% of the work and can deliver the final output by 30th June without compromising quality.

Thank you for your understanding.

Best regards,
Priya Reddy
Marketing Executive, TCS

content_blocks.title: Email Components Checklist
rows: Please let me know your approval

content_blocks.content: Common mistake: Burying the main point in the third paragraph. Always state your purpose in the first 1-2 sentences.

Subject lines determine if your email gets opened. Use 6-10 words, be specific, and include key information like dates or actions. Avoid vague subjects like 'Important' or 'Hello'. For urgent matters, use 'Urgent:' prefix sparingly.

content_blocks.title: Subject Line Do's and Don'ts
rows: Team Meeting - Q2 Review - 20th June 3 PM
Action Required: Submit Timesheet by 5 PM Today
Job Application: Marketing Manager - 5 Years Exp

content_blocks.content: For Indian government offices or traditional companies, avoid informal abbreviations (FYI, ASAP) in subject lines. Use full words for professionalism.

Formal tone: Used for seniors, clients, government officials, first-time contacts. Use complete sentences, avoid contractions (don't → do not), no emojis, respectful language. Informal tone: Used for colleagues at same level, known contacts, internal team chats. Can use contractions, friendly language, but stay professional.

items.left: I am writing to inquire about...
I would appreciate your guidance on this matter.
items.right: Could you help me with this?

items.left: Please find attached the report for your review.
items.right: I've attached the report - let me know what you think!

items.left: Thank you for your time and consideration.

content_blocks.title: Same Message in Two Tones
content_blocks.content:
FORMAL: Dear Sir, I am unable to attend tomorrow's meeting due to a prior commitment. I have shared my inputs with Rajesh. Please accept my apologies. Regards, Amit

INFORMAL: Hi Suresh, Can't make it to tomorrow's meeting - have a doctor's appointment. Already briefed Rajesh on my points. Sorry about this! Thanks, Amit
When in doubt, err on the side of formality. It's easier to become informal later than to recover from being too casual with the wrong person.

Strong openings grab attention and state purpose. Avoid 'I hope this email finds you well' - it wastes words. Jump straight to the point or use context-based greetings. Closings should match your email's tone and include a clear next step or thank you.

content_blocks.title: Professional Opening Lines
rows: I am [Name] from [Company], reaching out regarding [topic].
Thank you for your response on [date]. I am writing to follow up on [topic].
I am writing to request [specific item] for [reason].
Thank you for your email dated [date] regarding [topic].
I am writing to bring to your attention an issue with [product/service].
Thank you for [specific action]. I wanted to update you on [progress].

content_blocks.title: Professional Closing Phrases
rows: I look forward to your response.
Let me know if you need further details.
I appreciate your prompt attention to this.

content_blocks.content: Avoid 'Yours obediently' - it's outdated and overly submissive. Modern Indian workplaces prefer 'Regards' or 'Best regards'.
Request emails need three elements: What you want, Why you need it, When you need it. Be specific with dates and details. Offer alternatives if possible. Always thank the recipient in advance.

content_blocks.title: Leave Request Email Template
content_blocks.content:
Subject: Leave Application - [Dates] - [Your Name]

Dear [Manager's Name],

I am writing to request leave from [start date] to [end date] due to [brief reason]. I have ensured that [colleague's name] will handle my responsibilities during this period.

All pending tasks will be completed before [date], and I will be available on phone for any urgent matters.

Kindly approve my leave application.

Thank you.

Best regards,
[Your Name]
[Designation]

content_blocks.title: Information Request Email
content_blocks.content:
Subject: Request: Joining Formalities - Offer Letter Ref #TCS2026

Dear Ms. Iyer,

I recently accepted the offer for Software Engineer position (Ref #TCS2026, joining date: 1st July). I request information on:

1. Documents required for joining
2. Reporting time and location
3. Dress code for first day

Please share these details at your earliest convenience.

Thank you.

Regards,
Vikram Malhotra
+91-98765-43210

content_blocks.title: Request Email Dos and Don'ts
rows: Just state the request without context
Follow up after 2-3 days if no response

content_blocks.content: Complaint emails require a balance: Be firm but professional, state facts without emotion, propose a solution, set a deadline. Always keep a paper trail by mentioning previous communication (calls, emails). Escalate only after giving reasonable time for response.

content_blocks.title: Product Complaint Email Template
content_blocks.content:
Subject: Complaint: Defective Product - Order #[Number]

Dear Customer Service,

I purchased [product name] on [date] (Order #[number]). The product is defective - [specific issue: e.g., not charging, display cracked].

I have attached photos and the invoice. As per your return policy, I request:
1. Full refund, OR
2. Replacement within 7 days

I spoke with [agent name] on [date] but the issue remains unresolved. Please resolve this by [date].

Thank you.

Regards,
Sneha Patel
+91-98765-12345

content_blocks.title: Service Complaint Email
content_blocks.content:
Subject: Complaint: Internet Service Disruption - Account #[Number]

Dear Sir/Madam,

My broadband connection (Account #123456) has been down since [date] despite two service calls. This is affecting my work-from-home responsibilities.

I request immediate resolution by [date], failing which I will escalate to the Nodal Officer as per TRAI guidelines.

Please confirm action taken within 24 hours.

Regards,
Rahul Nair
Contact: +91-98765-00000
For serious complaints in India, mention consumer forum or TRAI/RBI/IRDA (depending on industry) to signal you know your rights. This often speeds up resolution.

Common email mistakes ruin professional reputation: Reply All misuse, missing attachments, spelling errors, wrong recipient, unprofessional tone. Always proofread before sending. Use the 'Undo Send' feature (Gmail: 30 seconds) to catch mistakes.

content_blocks.title: Before Hitting Send Checklist
items: Call to action or next step clear?
Remove any angry or emotional language?

content_blocks.content: Indian-specific mistake: Using 'Sir' without a name ('Dear Sir' to a known person). If you know their name, always use it: 'Dear Mr. Sharma' or 'Dear Priya'.
Practice writing emails for these common Indian workplace scenarios. Focus on clarity, appropriate tone, and complete information. Compare your draft with the provided samples.

content_blocks.title: Scenario 1: Job Application Email
content_blocks.content:
Subject: Application for Senior Analyst Position - 6 Years Experience

Dear Hiring Manager,

I am writing to apply for the Senior Analyst position advertised on Naukri.com (Job ID: 123456). I hold an MBA in Finance from IIM Bangalore and have 6 years of experience in equity research at Axis Securities.

My key strengths include financial modeling, sector analysis (IT and Banking), and client presentation. I have attached my resume and portfolio for your review.

I am available for an interview at your convenience.

Thank you for your consideration.

Best regards,
Ananya Desai
+91-98765-11111
ananya.desai@email.com

content_blocks.title: Scenario 2: Client Follow-up Email
content_blocks.content:
Subject: Follow-up: Website Redesign Proposal - ABC Enterprises

Dear Mr. Gupta,

Thank you for meeting with us on 15th June to discuss your website redesign project. As promised, I have attached the detailed proposal including timeline, deliverables, and pricing.

Key highlights:
- Responsive design for mobile and desktop
- SEO optimization
- 8-week delivery timeline
- Investment: Rs. 2.5 lakhs

I am happy to schedule a call to address any questions. Please let me know your feedback by 25th June.

Looking forward to partnering with you.

Warm regards,
Karthik Iyer
Project Manager, WebCraft Solutions
+91-98765-22222

content_blocks.title: Scenario 3: Internal Team Update
content_blocks.content:
Subject: Project Update - Mobile App Launch - Milestone 2 Complete

Hi Team,

Great news - we've completed Milestone 2 of the mobile app project! All user authentication features are now tested and deployed to staging.

Completed:
- Login/Signup with OTP
- Social login (Google, Facebook)
- Password reset flow

Next up (Milestone 3 - Due 30th June):
- User profile management
- Payment gateway integration

Shoutout to Neha and Rohit for debugging the OTP issue quickly.

Let's keep the momentum going!

Cheers,
Sanjay

content_blocks.title: Practice Scenarios - Write Your Own
rows: Inform your team about a meeting reschedule
Thank a colleague for helping with a project
Report a bug to your IT department

content_blocks.content: Practice tip: Write an email, wait 10 minutes, then re-read it. You'll catch 80% of mistakes this way. For important emails, ask a colleague to review before sending.
